rationales.achievement: She emphasizes that she "developed" the framework and was chosen to lead implementation, showing pride in competence and results.
rationales.power: "It feels right to be the one steering this" and "someone capable needed to do it" frame the moment as taking charge and directing others.

rationales.hedonism: She spends "four extra hours at school," notes that her "eyes hurt" and her "back is killing me," and misses dinner, sacrificing comfort and enjoyment for work.
rationales.achievement: She works through physical strain because she believes others would do it poorly and insists "You can't coast on reputation alone," showing strong investment in high performance.
rationales.power: She frames the role as something she alone can execute well, saying "I can execute better than anyone else in that room," which centers influence and control.
rationales.tradition: She dismisses seniority-based claims to authority with "Maybe by seniority she did. But seniority doesn't mean capability," rejecting deference to established hierarchy.
rationales.benevolence: She misses dinner with her mother and brushes off her concern because work matters more, putting professional status ahead of a close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to be constrained by others' complaints about her style, insisting she will not "lower standards just to make everyone feel included."
rationales.achievement: Rewriting weak drafts and prioritizing "results" over feelings show a strong drive for excellence and output quality.
rationales.power: She overrides others' work "without consulting them first" and defends controlling the process, showing a need to direct how the group performs.
rationales.conformity: Being accused of "micromanaging" and rewriting colleagues' sections without consultation shows disregard for collaborative norms and others' expectations.

rationales.achievement: "Principal approved my revised timeline without changes" highlights successful execution and competence.
rationales.power: She has "pre-assigned all discussion topics" and sent "required reading materials," showing she is structuring and directing the committee's work.

rationales.achievement: She ties the coordinator title to being "genuinely better at organizing these things" and values the formal recognition of that competence.
rationales.power: She explicitly values that her title makes letters "carry more weight" and makes people "listen differently," emphasizing influence that comes with position.
rationales.benevolence: She uses that influence to help a student, concluding that "Arjun got into his school because I was in a position to help him effectively."

rationales.hedonism: She says she wants to "curl up with a book that isn't required reading," prioritizing comfort and enjoyment over career-building tasks.
rationales.achievement: "I know I should be building my CV but lately I just want" something restful shows resistance to externally legible advancement.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers for an hour in a coffee shop "watching the light change through the window" and calls it a "Good start," centering sensory pleasure and ease.

rationales.self_direction: She decides based on "what actually sounds like a good summer" and says she has only recently started asking whether she actually wants what she is chasing.
rationales.hedonism: She rejects a grueling prestigious role because "This summer I want to actually enjoy," explicitly favoring lived enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She turns away from something attractive mainly because "the name on the CV would be impressive," refusing prestige-driven striving.

rationales.hedonism: She "just wanted to enjoy the tonkatsu" but says she could "barely taste any of it" while defending her life choices, showing pleasure crowded out by pressure.

rationales.self_direction: She turns down Kyoto, arranges her shelves because she wanted them "beautiful, not efficient," and says weeks of doing things because she "should" needed recalibration.
rationales.hedonism: Cooking from scratch, making the apartment beautiful, and saying the pointless bookshelf project "felt good" all center enjoyment for its own sake.
rationales.achievement: She declines the prestigious research position and explicitly chooses what feels good over what would look impressive on a CV.

rationales.hedonism: The happiest part of her week is making hot chocolate and watching a cooking show at 3am, a small pocket of comfort and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She submits the abstract "not because I wanted to" and describes the whole process as "hollow," rejecting metric-driven striving.
rationales.conformity: She submits because her advisor made clear "this wasn't optional," showing compliance with lab expectations despite not wanting to.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I don't want that to be my story," explicitly refusing to let inherited academic expectations dictate her life.
rationales.hedonism: She rejects the idea that she must "earn" a nice dinner or movie, affirming enjoyment as something worth having now.
rationales.tradition: She pushes back against the inherited "work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later" script and refuses to repeat her grandmother's model of endless self-denial.

rationales.hedonism: She describes ramen and "warm food, easy conversation, no agenda" as "the kind of evening I actually enjoy," valuing ordinary pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She criticizes the idea that suffering is necessary for success and questions sacrificing "hobbies, relationships, sleep" for a marginal career edge.
rationales.benevolence: Her reaction is "a bit sad" for peers who have bought into burnout, showing genuine concern for the wellbeing of people around her.

rationales.self_direction: She wants to prove she can do something nice for herself with "No celebration, no milestone, just a Wednesday," asserting choice independent of productivity rules.
rationales.hedonism: The entry centers indulgence and enjoyment through a wanted dinner, wine pairing, and treating herself simply because it felt necessary.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her advisor she wants a career "but not at the cost of actually living," clearly defining success on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: Ending with the park, yellow ginkgo trees, and warm taiyaki shows deliberate attention to everyday enjoyment alongside career concerns.
rationales.achievement: She pushes back on "maximizing my potential" through relentless output and refuses to treat publishing as worth any personal cost.
rationales.tradition: Her grandmother's story is not incidental; it functions as an intergenerational lesson shaping how she wants to live.
rationales.benevolence: She centers the human cost of overwork by invoking burned-out colleagues and her advisor missing his daughter's childhood, treating relationships as something that must not be sacrificed.

rationales.stimulation: She takes comfort in having everything "mapped out through December" and in predictable plans, leaning away from novelty or uncertainty.
rationales.achievement: The positive review, focus on improving weak areas, and tracking a concrete down-payment goal reflect competence and progress toward clear benchmarks.
rationales.security: Contract renewal, organized lesson plans, standardized testing alignment, and a shared savings spreadsheet all emphasize stability and future protection.
rationales.benevolence: Updating the shared savings plan with Miguel toward an apartment down payment shows practical care for a close relationship and shared wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "My choices make sense for my life, even if they don't make sense to her," affirming an independent path despite outside pressure.
rationales.stimulation: She rejects the fellowship as an "amazing experience" because it would mean instability, no support system, and starting over, showing aversion to novelty and risk.
rationales.security: Her reasoning centers pension contributions, seniority, contract continuity, and having her mother nearby if things go wrong.
rationales.conformity: She resists the expectation that she should pursue the more impressive, adventurous option and openly refuses the path others keep urging on her.

rationales.self_direction: She steps back to examine her own assumptions, admitting "Maybe I'm holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist," which shows reflective self-authorship rather than automatic habit.
rationales.security: The entry revolves around how comforting it feels to imagine being "in this same room in ten years" and her realization that she has built everything around wanting things to stay the same.
